item.title: teaching
item.description:
Some simulations and teaching materials. These are WIP; any corrections or suggestions are welcome.

  • Social Learning

item.title: hikes
item.description:
Some notes on hikes and backcountry situations.

  • Twin Peaks (3/26, SF): Pretty chill. About 900 feet elevation gain or so in 40 minutes. Pretty sure we saw a burglary in progress on the way down.

  • Chinatown to Chinatown to Chinatown Walk (11/25, NY): Not technically a hike, but a 20 mile Friendsgiving walk from Manhattan Chinatown to 8th Ave. Brooklyn to Flushing. Took breaks about every 3 miles on the second leg, near parks and coffeeshops. Nonlinear difference between this and the 32 mile perimeter one; this was quite manageable, but 32 mile one was disabling by the end. Got hotpot at the end.

item.title: research and simulations
item.description:
Working Papers

“Exploiting Symmetry in High-Dimensional Dynamic Programming.” (NBER) — Joint with Mahdi Ebrahimi Kahou, Jesus Fernandez-Villaverde, and Jesse Perla.

“Frequentist Persuasion” — Joint with James A. Best

Best Student Paper, AETW 2026

Some simulations: prosecutor-judge game and distributions over posteriors.

Works in Progress

“Socialized Learning”

item.title: writing
item.description:
For anything writing-related, please email writing@arnavsood.com

Fiction

“Customer Feedback (Secondhand Alchemical Goods)” — Daily Science Fiction.

  • Some reviews: Microverses and Reactor Mag

“Local Perturbation” — Daily Science Fiction.

item.title: links
item.description:
Some things I think are worth sharing.

  • Scientific American: North American bird populations have declined 29% since 1970.
  • NPR: As recently as 1960, fish used to be huge.
  • Green Your Lab: Good people working on sustainable science.
  • WIPP: How to warn people 10000 years from now about radioactive waste.
  • Sylvia Jacobson: Was confined to a skyjacked plane for a week, and wrote a paper about it.
  • Ken Liu: State Change (short story).
  • 129 Cars: This American Life (WBEZ Chicago) spends a month at a car dealership.
  • The Glass Room: A digital art exhibit by Mozilla, focused on examining issues of data and privacy.
  • Panopticlick: An EFF tool that lets you see how “unique” your browser’s fingerprint is.
  • Tor: An easy-to-use version of Firefox that effectively anonymizes your internet traffic.
  • The MIT License, Line by Line: /dev/lawyer does a close-reading of the common MIT License.
  • Kalle Lasn: “Cleaning Up the Mental Environment.” Lasn is the founder/publisher of Vancouver-based Adbusters.
  • Wipr: Highly effective macOS adblocker. I use this.
  • Gather.Town: 8bit online spaces. I invested in this.
  • Concentration of Measure. People use this word and we should know what it means. It’s more than Hoeffding or DKW or something. Bobkov’s inequality looks profound but above my level.
  • Surviving First Year. Good advice.
  • Multivariate Delta Method + Glivenko-Cantelli Classes. I worked with GC classes for a year but it never sunk in until I saw section 8.4.

Model · Informational Cascades Bikhchandani, Hirshleifer & Welch (1992) · Smith & Sørensen (2000)

Agents  N 40

ready

AI Clones — Figure 2
The platform observes representation distance, not true distance
Liang 2026
Model
Target x₀ at origin. Candidates Xᵢ drawn from the unit ball. Each interaction generates noisy reps Yᵢ₀ and Y₀ᵢ. Platform selects argmin ‖Yᵢ₀ − Y₀ᵢ‖.
Dimensions
Parameters
AI Noise σ 0.15
Candidates n 3
Show / Hide
True distances
Rep. distances
Noise connectors
Distance labels
Legend
x₀ target
Xᵢ true candidate
Yᵢ₀ candidate rep
Y₀ᵢ target rep
true dist ‖Xᵢ‖
rep dist ‖Yᵢ₀−Y₀ᵢ‖
platform's pick
Distances
hide all
—

Frequentist Persuasion c_N(z) · π(z) · V_N(z) as functions of lying probability
Parameters
μ₀ = Pr[guilty] 0.30
N (sample size) 20
Benchmarks
z^KG
—
μ₀ / (1−μ₀) · limit of z*(N)
c_N(z^KG)
—
converges to ½ from above
c_N(z) = Pr[convict] · faint = π(z) = Pr[m=guilty]
V_N(z) = π(z) · c_N(z) — prosecutor value

Frequentist Persuasion Receiver's Frequentist Welfare
Parameters
mu0 = Pr[guilty] 0.30
z (lying prob) 0.25
N (sample size) 20
Legend
A = credible (convict)
B = non-credible (acquit)
C = defective (acquit)
Values
U^F_N(z)
-
A = Pr[credible]
-
B = Pr[non-credible]
-
C = Pr[defective]
-
A\(\,\cdot\,(1-(1-\mu_0)z)\)
—
credible · convict
+
\((\)B\(+\)C\()\,\cdot\,(1-\mu_0)\)
—
non-credible + defective · acquit
=
\(U^F_N(z)\)
—
receiver welfare
outcome probabilities with posterior distribution overlay

Frequentist Persuasion Receiver welfare heatmap in (z, N) space
Parameters
mu0 = Pr[guilty]0.30
Colour scale
— U^F —
Reference
z^KG = mu0/(1-mu0)
—
min z*(N) · at N
—
U^F at min z*
—
max U^F on z*(N) · at N
—
U^F at max
—
exact U^F_N(z) · z: 1 to 0 · N: log scale 1 to 500 · dashed = z^KG · white curve = z*(N)
COMPUTING

Frequentist Persuasion Distribution of posteriors given m = guilty
Parameters
μ₀ = Pr[guilty] 0.30
z (lying prob) 0.25
Pr[m = guilty | θ = innocent]
N (sample size) 8
Summary
z^KG
—
μ₀ / (1−μ₀)
Pr[convict] = c_N(z)
—
weight of points with posterior ≥ ½
π(z) = Pr[m = guilty]
—
V_N(z) = π · c_N
—
Each point = one (X,Y) pair with X+Y > 0 · x-axis: posterior X/(X+Y) · y-axis: Pr[X=x, Y=y]

Frequentist Persuasion

Game Simulator Sood & Best · 2026
Signal structure
Prior μ₀ 0.40
Pr[θ = guilty]
Lying prob z 0.40
Pr[guilty | θ = innocent]
Sampling
Sample size K
iid draws before the judge decides
Controls
Prosecutor
Signal structure σ
↑ prosecutor's private information
↓ judge observes
Judge
Sample — iid draws from Gσ,μ₀
0 X  (guilty, guilty)
0 Y  (innocent, guilty)
0 Z  (innocent, innocent)
# θ  (true state) m  (reported)
You are the judge
After the sample is drawn, a fresh (θ, m) pair arrives. You see only m. Decide; then see the payoffs.
waiting…
Payoffs
Prosecutor
—
1 if judge convicts, else 0
Judge
—
1 if verdict matches true state, else 0

Frequentist Persuasion Optimal lying probability z*(N)
Prior
μ₀ = Pr[guilty] 0.30
Prior probability defendant is guilty
Benchmarks
KG optimum z^KG
—
μ₀ / (1−μ₀), capped at 1
Min z* over K shown
—
achieved at K = —

K max 120
